planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring; despite being a wetland tree, bald cypress performs well in ordinary, well-drained soil — it does not require wet conditions to thrive. Plant 6–10 feet from structures to allow for its natural buttressed base.

directions: Plant in full sun in any moist to wet or even ordinary well-drained soil; bald cypress is the defining tree of the Deep South and Gulf Coast swamps, but contrary to popular belief it grows and thrives in ordinary garden soil without standing water. The feathery, pale green foliage on horizontal branches turns brilliant copper-orange in fall before dropping — making this an unexpectedly beautiful deciduous conifer for the landscape. In wet soil the trees produce distinctive 'knees' (woody projections from the root system) that emerge from the water; in normal garden soil, knees are usually minimal or absent. The tree is extremely long-lived (centuries to millennia in the wild), remarkably storm-resistant, and grows 1–2 feet per year when young. An excellent large-scale specimen or street tree in the South.

planting.note: Plant bareroot seedlings in fall or winter in sandy, well-drained, infertile, acidic soil; longleaf pine establishes best from small seedlings planted in fall. Do not fertilize or amend soil — longleaf requires poor, sandy conditions. Mow around young plants to control competing vegetation.

directions: Plant bareroot seedlings in fall or winter in sandy, infertile, acidic, extremely well-drained soil in full sun; longleaf pine is one of the most ecologically critical trees in North America, once covering 90 million acres of the southeastern coastal plain but now reduced to less than 3% of its original range — one of the continent's most severe ecological losses. Planting longleaf is an act of ecological restoration. The tree is famous for its 'grass stage' — for 3–7 years, the seedling looks like a grass clump with no visible stem while it develops a deep taproot; then growth accelerates dramatically. Mature longleaf lives 300–500 years, supporting 29 endangered species including the red-cockaded woodpecker, indigo snake, and gopher tortoise. The long needles (12–18 inches), massive cones, and fire-adapted bark make it an outstanding specimen tree in appropriate sandy soils.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist soil; avoid alkaline soils which cause chlorosis. Choose sterile (fruitless) cultivars like 'Rotundiloba' if the spiky seed balls are undesirable.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun; sweetgum is one of the most common and beautiful deciduous trees of the eastern US, producing perhaps the most spectacular and varied fall color display of any native tree — individual trees vary from pure yellow to orange to red to deep burgundy-purple, and many trees display all these colors simultaneously on the same tree. The star-shaped leaves are distinctive and beautiful. The 'gumballs' (spiky spherical seed capsules) are the tree's only liability — the seeds provide important food for finches and small rodents but the fallen capsules can be a nuisance underfoot. Sterile cultivars like 'Rotundiloba' produce no seeds and have rounder leaf lobes. The tree grows 1–2 feet per year and is exceptionally adaptable to a wide range of conditions. The aromatic resin (storax) has traditional medicinal and fragrance uses.

planting.note: Plant bareroot seedlings in fall or winter in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic soil; loblolly pine is one of the fastest-growing southern pines (2–4 feet per year when young) and establishes readily from small seedlings.

directions: Plant bareroot seedlings or container trees in fall or early spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; loblolly pine is the most common and commercially important tree of the South, but it is also a critical wildlife tree — the seeds are consumed by dozens of bird species including red-cockaded woodpeckers (which nest exclusively in mature pines), white-breasted nuthatches, and pine siskins, while the open structure of mature pine canopy supports a rich understory community of native plants. It grows extremely fast (2–4 feet per year) and provides rapid screening, wind protection, and habitat structure. The three long needles in bundles and heavy cones are distinctive. Susceptible to pine bark beetles when stressed by drought; plant in appropriate moist, sandy soils and avoid planting far outside its natural range.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; sweetleaf is not commonly available in nurseries — look for it from native plant specialists and botanic gardens.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; sweetleaf (also called horse sugar) is a native shrub or small tree of moist woodland slopes and stream banks in the southeastern US, best known for three remarkable characteristics: the leaves have a distinctly sweet taste (hence both common names), deer browse the sweet foliage voraciously; the bright yellow flower clusters in late winter to early spring appear before most other shrubs and provide crucial early-season nectar for queen bumblebees emerging from overwintering; and it is the exclusive larval host plant for Henry's elfin butterfly, a small, understated but increasingly rare butterfly that depends entirely on this plant for its caterpillars. Underused in horticulture, sweetleaf deserves much wider planting for its early nectar value and butterfly support.
